reference_answer: File B-423552.3; issued March 24, 2026.

Protester: Enviremedial Services, Inc. (ESI), a small business of Oceanside, California. Awardee: BryMak & Associates, Inc., a small business of Clarksville, Tennessee. Agency: Department of the Army, Corps of Engineers.
RFP No. W912HP25R1000. The solicitation contemplated the award of a fixed-price, indefinite-delivery, indefinite-quantity contract for a 12-month base period and four 12-month option periods.
Anuj Vohra, Esq., Issac D. Schabes, Esq., and Emily P. Golchini, Esq., of Crowell & Moring LLP.
Lucas T. Hanback, Esq., Jules L. Szanton, Esq., and Timothy A. Wieroniey, Esq., of Rogers Joseph O'Donnell, PC, for BryMak & Associates, Inc.
Christopher Alwood, Esq., and Alexander O. Levine, Esq.
Edda Emmanuelli Perez, General Counsel.
The Corps issued the RFP on February 10, 2025; the closing date for proposals was March 13.
Enviremedial Servs., Inc., B-423552, B-423552.2, decided August 28, 2025.
The Corps notified ESI of the post-corrective action award on December 3; ESI filed this protest on December 17, 2025.
Within 10 days of when the basis for reconsideration is known or should have been known, under 4 C.F.R. § 21.14(b) (with the right to request reconsideration provided by 4 C.F.R. § 21.14(a)).

$356,277, or 2.1 percent, in BryMak's favor. The SSA concluded that, in the absence of material discriminators between the proposals under either non-price factor, BryMak represented the best value to the government as the lower-priced proposal.

(1) Corps issues RFP (February 10, 2025); (2) prior GAO decision (August 28, 2025); (3) agency notice of post-corrective action award (December 3); (4) ESI files this protest (December 17, 2025); (5) this decision (March 24, 2026).

Understanding of the Work: BryMak Good / ESI Outstanding. Past Performance — Relevancy: BryMak Very Relevant / ESI Very Relevant. Past Performance — Confidence: BryMak Substantial Confidence / ESI Substantial Confidence. Price: BryMak $16,754,056 / ESI $17,110,333.

1. The protest challenging the agency's evaluation of past performance relevance is dismissed as untimely where the protester raised the protest ground in a prior protest, our Office denied it, and the protester did not timely request reconsideration of our prior decision. 2. The protest challenging the agency's evaluation of past performance is denied where the evaluation was reasonable and consistent with the solicitation's terms. 3. The protest challenging the agency's best-value tradeoff is denied where the agency's tradeoff was reasonable, adequately documented, and consistent with the terms of the solicitation.
Very relevant, relevant, somewhat relevant, or not relevant.
Substantial confidence, satisfactory confidence, limited confidence, no confidence, or unknown confidence.

A relevant contract was defined as a contract that was ongoing or completed within 5 years of the RFP's closing date; was fundamentally for facilities preventative maintenance, repair work, and service calls; and had a contract value of no less than $400,000 per year.
18 sites in Georgia, North Carolina, and South Carolina, in support of the United States Army Reserve's 81st Readiness Division.

The prior protest (Enviremedial Servs., B-423552, B-423552.2, Aug. 28, 2025) was sustained in part — GAO found certain aspects of the agency's relevancy evaluation unreasonable or not sufficiently documented and the best-value tradeoff was based on an unreasonable past performance evaluation. This protest (B-423552.3) was denied (with one ground dismissed as untimely).
The agency assigned the same adjectival ratings as in the first evaluation and calculated the same total evaluated prices.

GAO noted: "We note that the protester raises several collateral arguments. While our decision does not specifically address every argument, we have reviewed all the arguments and conclude that none provides a basis to sustain the protest."

Because GAO had reviewed that allegation in the prior protest and found it did not provide a basis to sustain; the agency's reevaluation did not change or address that aspect; the arguments raised were substantially similar to those raised earlier and did not rely on any new information gleaned as part of the reevaluation; and ESI did not file a request for reconsideration within the 10-day deadline under 4 C.F.R. § 21.14(b). Accordingly the repetitive allegation amounted to an untimely request for reconsideration that GAO declined to consider.

First, in eAlliant the agency removed strengths without explanation, whereas here the agency's underlying evaluation of the aspect of ESI's proposal at issue did not change (both the pre- and post-corrective action SSEB reports specifically stated that one ESI contract "had [three] marginal ratings on a 6-month interim CPAR evaluation"). Second, unlike in eAlliant, GAO had sustained the original protest in part on the basis that the agency had failed to look behind the adjectival ratings and qualitatively compare the relative merits of the offerors' past performance — so the SSA's actions here were viewed not as changing evaluation conclusions but as performing the required qualitative comparison for the first time.

ESI's excerpt of the recommendation that the agency "reevaluate proposals in a manner consistent with the terms of the solicitation" omitted the end of the sentence: "and this decision." GAO found the omission significant because that language limited the recommended reevaluation to the errors identified in the prior decision rather than recommending a completely new evaluation, and therefore ESI could have requested reconsideration of the protest grounds GAO denied or dismissed.
